conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. 

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances. Let me start by learning about your situation:

Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you asking about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have two children - Alex, who is 17, and Blake, who is 3. I live here in the UK and they both live with me. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information! It's helpful to know you have two children, Alex (17) and Blake (3), and that you all live together in the UK.

Before I can assess your eligibility, I need to read the official guidance on Child Benefit rules. Let me do that now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. Now I have reviewed the official guidance. I need to ask a few more questions to fully assess your eligibility for both Alex and Blake.

For Alex, who is 17 years old, I need to know: **Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** (This could include things like A-levels, college courses, or certain training programs)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment. They finished school and aren't doing any courses right now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I have a follow-up question about Alex:

**Has Alex left education or training within the last 20 weeks?** If so, has Alex registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex left school more than 20 weeks ago and hasn't registered with any careers service or anything like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I have one more question about Alex's current situation:

**Is Alex currently working, and if so, how many hours per week?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't working at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for all that information. Now I just need to confirm a couple of things about Blake to complete my assessment:

**Is Blake (who is 3 years old) currently in hospital, in care, or in any other living arrangement, or does Blake live with you full-time at home?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake lives with me full-time at home. No hospital or care or anything like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have everything I need to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

Let me provide you with my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Assessment Complete**

Based on the information you've provided and the official Child Benefit rules, here's my determination:

**For Blake (3 years old): ✓ ELIGIBLE**
You can claim Child Benefit for Blake. Since Blake is under 16 and lives with you in the UK, you meet all the eligibility requirements.

**For Alex (17 years old): ✗ NOT ELIGIBLE**
Unfortunately, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Alex. While Child Benefit can continue for children aged 16-19, they must be in approved education or training. Since Alex is not currently in education or training, left school more than 20 weeks ago, and hasn't registered with a careers service or the armed services, the eligibility criteria are not met.

**Important note:** If Alex's circumstances change - for example, if Alex starts an approved education course, training program, or registers with a careers service - you may become eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex at that point. You would need to inform the Child Benefit Office of any such changes.

You can proceed with claiming Child Benefit for Blake.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 3 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Child is not in local authority care

Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 3 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and is not currently in approved education or training. According to the Child Benefit eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit for a child under 16. For children aged 16 or over, you'll still qualify if they are under 20 AND stay in approved education or training. Additionally, Child Benefit can continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Alex left education more than 20 weeks ago, is not in approved education or training, and has not registered with a careers service or armed services. Alex is also not working. Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 3 years old, which is under 16 years old. Blake lives with you full-time in the UK, and you are responsible for Blake. Blake is not in hospital or care. According to the Child Benefit eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. You'll usually be responsible for a child if you live with them. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (17) and Blake (3). Both children live with the claimant in the UK. Blake meets the basic eligibility criteria as a child under 16 living with the claimant. However, Alex does not meet the eligibility criteria because, although Alex is under 20, Alex is not in approved education or training, left education more than 20 weeks ago without registering with a careers service or armed services, and is not working. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Blake only.

Alex left education more than 20 weeks ago, is not in approved education or training, and has not registered with a careers service or armed services. Alex is also not working. Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 3 years old, which is under 16 years old. Blake lives with you full-time in the UK, and you are responsible for Blake. Blake is not in hospital or care. According to the Child Benefit eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. You'll usually be responsible for a child if you live with them. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.

Blake meets the basic eligibility criteria as a child under 16 living with the claimant. However, Alex does not meet the eligibility criteria because, although Alex is under 20, Alex is not in approved education or training, left education more than 20 weeks ago without registering with a careers service or armed services, and is not working. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Blake only.
